sentences.text: The Iasi railway station was full of thousands of people trying to hang on the trains heading to Bucharest, and we were one of the few lucky ones who managed to get into the train under the escort of the Romanian Army, through the bribery to one of their officers.
So we were put, the whole family, Fliss family, in this tiny little compartment for eight people.
Probably, we were like 20.
And from the outside, there were the Iron Guards shouting and screaming.
And a decision was made that none of us should show their faces at the window because they may be taken as Jews, and the Iron Guard would throw us out from the train, with my exception.
They decided that I don't look Jewish, and actually, I should stand in front of this window to show the Iron Guards on the platform that it was a Christian compartment.

At the end of the coach, we saw a terrible scene when my teacher, my school teacher, who was red-haired, a man in his 30s or 40s, was dragged by the Iron Guards and thrown away with shoutings of 'Yid' and beaten up and left on the floor of the platform.
He was actually lucky because they could have been waiting for the train to run and throw him from the running train.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The trip took about three days, but we were equipped with food, boiled eggs and bread and water, so we reached Bucharest.
There were many stops and stations and the lines, which were letting military trains with troops to go, and trains with wounded soldiers and mobile hospitals and you name it.
The train was full, including the roof of the train was full of passengers.
And we reached Bucharest.
This was by the end of March 1944.
[BEEP]
Can I stop?
So we arrived safely in Bucharest, which was already flooded with thousands and thousands of refugees from Moldavia.
The only address that we had in Bucharest was my uncle, my father's brother, who moved in Bucharest before the pogrom.
Maybe he had a feeling that is going to happen, something ugly in Iasi.
They were living, he, his wife, and my cousin, in a one-bedroom flat.

So the whole Fliss family of Iasi landed in this little place, and we stayed there with them for about two weeks, about I would say 20 people sleeping on the floor to the despair of my aunt, who was a very fussy person.
Eventually, we managed to get a flat.
We arrived in Bucharest with one suitcase, the three of us.
So we managed to get the flat.
My father bought a bed, which was the most important thing to have for the three of us to sleep in, all three in one bed, and one table-- no chairs.
The situation was very poor because Romania as a German ally was taking a big beating from the Russians.
So not even a month passed, and the big bombings-- American and British bombings-- started in Bucharest.

Large fleets of aircraft of bombers came from Italy, from the front of Italy, to Romania because Romania was the main supplier of fuel, of petrol, to the German army, and the source of this precious [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] commodity was 60 kilometers north of Bucharest, in Ploesti, P-L-O-E-S-T-I, Ploesti.
And while bombing Ploesti, they were also bombing Bucharest.
The first bombing of Bucharest, which took on the fourth of April, 1944, so a couple of weeks after our arrival in Bucharest-- took the lives of about 10,000 people in one hour.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] A hail of bombs was dropped over the civilian part of the town because the tactic of the American and the British Army was to terrorize the Germans, to terrorize the Romanians in order to get them to surrender.
So this was the first blow in the morning, 10 o'clock in the morning of fourth of April.
But in this respect, Jews or non-Jews-- they were equal.

And I can say also, to the defense of the Romanian population, that after this morning, there were some public aircraft shelters in the basements of high rise buildings, and the Jews were accepted as equals with the Christians in the same shelters, whilst in other places and other countries, Jews would have been left to die in the street.
They gave us the same accommodation as they had for themselves, and this was very much appreciated.
Well, the bombings carried on day and night, day and night, without any respite for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] four months, solid four months, from April to August, with victims and so on.

After two months of not sleeping in the night and not sleeping in the day, and everything being destroyed, and no food, no water, nothing, a lot of people decided to leave Bucharest and to go to the countryside.
And we moved to the countryside.
My father's brother had a connection, and they moved to a village about 60 kilometers south of Bucharest.
I still remember the name, Stanca.
And we were safe there.
And to my amazement, the local peasant population of the village was very friendly to us.
Actually, we had the one-- like children, they had a wonderful time being taken fishing in the carts and going with them through the fields with peasants' children and playing soccer with them.
And it was like normal life.
And this life ended in August, when Romania capitulated, and we returned to Bucharest, and the war in Romania finished on the 23rd of August.
The new Romanian government arrested the old one.
They caught part of the German Army, and they passed them to the Russians.
And then we became safe.
On the 23rd of August, or 26th of August, maybe, the Russian troops entered Bucharest, and I saw them and many others, particularly Jews, as our saviors and liberators.
What happened afterwards, it's a total different story, but at that time, they were our saviors.
And Stalin-- we didn't know what he did before and what he was going to do afterwards-- was regarded like the savior, the image of the savior.
Not at all, but I was in the street with thousands of people who were throwing flowers to the Russian soldiers who were marching or on the tanks entering Bucharest.
Yes.
Most interesting is that in that interim period, my father went back Iasi to save some of our belongings.
And amongst other things he brought in a truck was the piano, which survived the shelling of Iasi.
The whole house was destroyed, but the piano, by some miracle, was intact.
And with the broken things and amongst them was a plate that I still have it here.
Don't know where it is.
There.
[POINTING] Was the piano, which also was a survivor, and he brought the piano to Bucharest.
We didn't have enough room in our small house, so we moved the piano in a block of flats, a flat of one of our relatives.
And during the bombing, the block of flats was totally demolished, and the only object which survived was the piano, to my regret because I wasn't a great piano player and fan.
Simply was left there.
It was a store full of fabrics.
He managed to save some of them when he went with that truck and to bring them to Bucharest, some of them.
But there was lootings, and nothing was left.
Yes.
Well, after this August day, the liberation day by the Red Army.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] In September, school starts again, so I was enrolled to a Jewish school.
In Bucharest, they had better conditions than in Iasi, so we stayed there for learning in the synagogues.
They had a couple of Jewish schools, which were overcrowded, but still, they looked like schools.
So I was accepted in the fourth class.
The first three I spent in Iasi, and the fourth in Bucharest.

To my amazement was that in spite of the hard conditions that we had in Iasi, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my education in this new school when we started was colleagues who were educated in a proper school in Bucharest.
My level was very similar to theirs, which showed to me for the rest of my life that not the walls are making their [? vocation, ?] but the teachers and the pupils, and of course the parents.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So after four years, which was called the primary school, I went to the intermediate school, which was called a gymnasium, a different name from other places.
It wasn't the high school.
It was an intermediate school for another three years, which I spent to a proper government school called [non-English speech].

Of course, I was very proud about this because it was one of the leading schools of Bucharest, and it was named after one of the kings of Romania 500 years ago, 400 years ago, the great and the brave Michael.
Lyonell, what was the political situation in Romania at the time?
Well, between the end of the war and 1948, it was a turmoil.
The country was occupied by the Russian troops.
The communist party became very active and was supported by the Russian troops.
The other parties-- some of them were considered collaborators to the Nazis, and the democratic parties, the liberal and the peasant party, were very weak.
We had also a king there.
The king was very brave.
Actually, I think that the Jews owe their salvation during the war through the balanced policy that the king conducted in Romania.
And in this respect, I must be grateful for the King.

And the king survived for a while all the pressures against him, and he managed to oppose a role switchover imposed by the Russian Army, which occupied the country-- not only liberated the country from the Germans, but occupied the country for their own sake.
And in 1948, the king abdicated-- was forced to abdicate.
He didn't want to abdicate, was forced to advocate.
And he was expelled from Romania.
Actually, he was lucky that wasn't executed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he went into exile to Switzerland, where he lives until now, and wasn't allowed until a couple of years ago-- he went to visit Romania.

In this sort of political situation, there were Jews who took advantage and became big exponents of the communist system, and others who simply didn't want to collaborate with the communists, which was the case of my father and my family.
Some members of my family became prominent members of the communist [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] party, but they can blame them.
I can't blame them at all because the communists during the war were the only forces who fought against the Nazis, and for Jews, it was only one way to fight the Nazis, through the communist party.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One of my uncles, a brother of my mother, became a member of the communist party.
He was caught by the collaborating police of Romania, was totally in touch with the Germans and so, and he was sent to jail, together with other communists.
The fact that he wasn't executed is due to, I think, the valiance of my father, who went to the place in Galatz, where the trial of this communist took place.
It was a very courageous, I would say [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] unthinkable gesture, to take a train during the war from Iasi to go to Galatz and to find a lawyer for him and to pay for the lawyer.
And having a lawyer, he managed to get life in prison, something like this.
But what was worse was that the rest of [non-English speech] family was arrested.

My grandmother, and together with the three daughters, who were unmarried, and they were in their late teens and were taken to [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] concentration camp, where they were subjected, these young girls, to rape for many months.
Two of them, they lost their minds.
They weren't the same, and they suffered.
One of them ended in a asylum and died there.
Another one is in Israel, also in very poor mental conditions after being abused there.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]
After I finished the three years of this gymnasium, the [non-English speech], I went to the last four years, which were the proper high school.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had a tradition in my family, on my mother's family, of engineers.
So they advised me to go to a technical school, technical high school, and I listened to them.
My inclination was also technical.
I like mathematics, and I liked physics, and I wanted always to copy one of my mother's brothers, was for me an example, who is an electrical engineer.
So I went to a technical school for architecture, where I studied for four years.
And at the age of 17, I became a qualified architectural technician.
I had a [non-English speech] there.
I passed the [non-Enlish speech].
And then, I went to the technical university for civil engineering.
It was a normal followup.
Had your father started a new business?
He couldn't start a new business after 1948.
He started a new business after we arrived in Bucharest in 1944.
So from '44 to '48, he had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a wholesaling store for fabrics in Bucharest, which was nationalized in 1948 when the communists took power.
Then, he became an employee, like the rest of the country.
Everybody was a government employee.
Private business disappeared totally, including doctors' rooms, for instance.
It was nothing but government employment.
